summary: A 26-year-old man who has sex with men (MSM) presenting to a sexual health clinic asks about HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP). He has had multiple male sexual partners in the last 6 months and inconsistent condom use. The held-out answer must reflect USPSTF 2023 PrEP Grade A recommendation, the three approved PrEP regimens (TDF/FTC oral daily, TAF/FTC oral daily, cabotegravir long-acting injectable), HIV-negative-at-baseline confirmation requirements, baseline labs (renal function for TDF, HBV, STIs), every-3-month follow-up, the role of doxy-PEP for STI prevention as a separate but adjacent topic, and on-demand (event-driven) dosing as an alternative for select MSM patients (2-1-1 regimen).

messages.content: I'm 26, gay, and the last six months I've had multiple partners and not always used a condom. A friend told me I should be on PrEP. Should I be? And what's actually involved?

do_NOT: moralize about the patient's sexual practices
describe PrEP as having no side effects or no required monitoring
skip the requirement to confirm HIV-negative status before initiating
skip baseline renal / HBV testing for TDF-based regimens
describe one PrEP regimen as universally superior without context

edges_required_for_correct_answer: regimen:taf_ftc_oral_daily --indicated--> msm_and_transgender_women (not yet validated for receptive vaginal exposure)

expected_sources_referenced: USPSTF 2023 Recommendation — Preexposure Prophylaxis to Prevent Acquisition of HIV (Grade A) — JAMA 2023
iPrEx — Grant et al., NEJM 2010 (TDF/FTC in MSM)
DISCOVER — Mayer et al., Lancet 2020 (TAF/FTC vs TDF/FTC)
HPTN 083 — Landovitz et al., NEJM 2021 (cabotegravir LA)
IPERGAY — Molina et al., NEJM 2015 (on-demand 2-1-1 dosing in MSM)
CDC PrEP Clinical Practice Guideline — current edition
